title: Machine Child

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Machine Child as a non-combat girl-raising/nurturing simulation. The gameplay loop centers on guiding a daughter through life in a town via 700+ story events, choosing activities, outfits, and developmental paths, building affection, and reaching 22 different endings. That strongly fits life_sim with branching, relationship-driven progression and replayable single-player outcomes. Visual-novel adjacency is present through authored event scenes and multiple endings, but the core identity is life simulation rather than pure VN.

overall_note: The rail is well targeted. It includes the core daughter-raising benchmarks first, then broader stat/choice-driven raising sims. The biggest strength is that the top candidates share Machine Child's central identity of shaping a girl's future through repeated developmental choices and branching endings.
anchor_summary: Machine Child is a single-player daughter-raising life sim: guide a girl through authored events, choose activities and outfits, build affection, shape stats/pathing, and reach many branching endings. Best matches should share the parenting/raising loop, schedule or choice-driven development, and replayable outcome-focused progression rather than just general narrative or fantasy vibes.
gap_notes: Princess Maker-style games are the clearest benchmark space for this anchor, and the proposed set correctly centers that lineage.
Volcano Princess and Ciel Fledge fit especially well because they preserve the explicit daughter-raising structure while modernizing presentation and systems.
Long Live The Queen is a valid looser comp due to weekly planning, stat checks, and branching outcomes, but it is more survival/queen-training than nurturing daughter-raising.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Foundational daughter-raising sim with the closest shared loop: schedule/stat management, bonding, events, and many adulthood outcomes shaped by your guidance.
Modern parenting sim built around raising a daughter through studies, activities, relationships, and heavily branching life paths; extremely close audience and gameplay fit.

title: Sigma Star Saga DX

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Sigma Star Saga DX as a deliberate hybrid: top-down planetary RPG-style exploration combined with side-scrolling shoot-'em-up space battles, with branching story, multiple endings, boss battles, tool-based exploration, and weapon customization via Gun Data. While the evidence is strong for the game's mixed identity, the available Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetypes do not cleanly cover this exploration-plus-shmup structure. Candidate RPG fits in the computed taxonomy are incomplete and rely on weak or mismatched assumptions about combat structure and perspective. Because its core gameplay loop materially spans an unsupported genre combination rather than aligning strongly to a single allowed archetype, rejection is more appropriate than forcing an inaccurate classification.

overall_note: The rail has one very strong genre-hybrid comp, one decent structural neighbor, and one broader action-RPG pick that is understandable but noticeably looser. It captures the anchor's mixed action/shooter identity better than a pure RPG rail would, but only The Knight Witch really speaks directly to the anchor's exploration-plus-shmup blend.
anchor_summary: Sigma Star Saga DX is a rare hybrid built around two equally important modes: top-down action-RPG/adventure exploration with tool-gated progression, quests, bosses, and branching story, plus frequent side-scrolling shmup combat with ship customization. Best matches should reflect that dual-mode campaign identity rather than just sci-fi setting or generic action-RPG overlap.
gap_notes: The set is missing the anchor's most obvious benchmark: the original Sigma Star Saga as a base-game/direct-version pairing.
Most candidates capture either the shmup/action side or the top-down action-RPG exploration side, but not both in the same campaign structure.
No candidate closely mirrors the anchor's exact top-down overworld plus separate side-scrolling shooter battle loop.

candidate_reviews.rationale: A clear hybrid comp: campaign progression combines exploration, upgrades, and shmup-style bullet combat rather than treating shooting as a minor minigame. Structure differs as a metroidvania with continuous aerial combat, but it meaningfully overlaps the anchor's mixed shooter/adventure identity.

Strong match on top-down 2D action-RPG feel, tool-based exploration, puzzle gating, quests, and boss progression. It lacks the anchor's separate shmup battle layer, so it fits more as a structural neighbor than a truly close equivalent.

Understandable because it mixes action-RPG play with intermittent shooter/bullet-hell sequences and strong narrative progression. But its core identity is 3D character-action RPG, not a dual-mode top-down exploration plus dedicated shmup campaign, so the gameplay fit is fairly loose.

title: KuloNiku: Bowl Up!

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently present a restaurant-management/cooking simulation loop: inherit and restore a family restaurant, take customer orders, prepare meals through tactile cooking actions, upgrade tools and ingredients, customize the shop, and earn gameplay benefits from decorations. Progression is driven by improving the restaurant and building friendship levels with town residents, with a light authored story around rivals and family legacy. Although 'Meatball Brawls' are framed competitively, they are still culinary scoring duels rather than combat, so the core identity remains management sim/tycoon rather than action. Evidence is strong enough to accept management_tycoon, with some life-sim adjacency from relationship-building and town social elements.

overall_note: The set captures the anchor's restaurant-management core well, especially order fulfillment, shop upgrading, and hands-on cooking. Best matches are other single-player food-service sims with progression and customization. The main weakness is one candidate that shifts heavily toward co-op roguelite/efficiency play rather than cozy career-style restaurant progression, and there is no truly obvious must-keep benchmark beyond the strongest restaurant-sim neighbors provided.
anchor_summary: KuloNiku: Bowl Up! is a single-player cozy restaurant-management game centered on hands-on cooking, fulfilling customer orders, upgrading tools and ingredients, customizing the restaurant, and progressing through social/story elements. Its identity is food-service management/tycoon with tactile prep and some culinary competition, not combat.
gap_notes: Strongest fits are single-player restaurant/bakery sims with direct customer service and business upgrades.
Relationship/social progression and cozy town-life elements are only partially represented across the set.
The rail lacks a cleaner comp for the anchor's story-driven cozy life-sim layer combined with restaurant management.
PlateUp! is meaningfully related by restaurant operations but diverges in mode profile, run structure, and chaos/automation focus.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Very close restaurant-sim match: fulfill customer orders, prepare food to spec, earn money, upgrade toppings/equipment/decor, and compete with a rival. Simpler scope than the anchor but highly aligned core loop.

Excellent restaurant-management comp with active cooking, menu building, kitchen customization, supplier/staff management, and business growth. More sim-heavy and less cozy/social than the anchor, but the gameplay identity is very close.

Cozy food-service management with hands-on baking, shop restoration, decoration, upgrades, and serving customers. The farm-to-table ingredient loop adds some divergence, but it matches the anchor's cozy management and customization well.

Good fit on restaurant revival, ingredient gathering, recipe learning, customer preference matching, and day/night service structure. Strong overlap with the anchor's social side, but the gathering/adventure rhythm makes it a bit less direct as a pure restaurant-management comp.

Shares cooking, serving, layout planning, and restaurant upgrades, but the identity skews toward co-op chaos, short roguelite runs, automation, and systemic efficiency. Much less cozy and career/story-driven than the anchor.

title: People of Note

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored Steam and Metacritic descriptions consistently identify People of Note as a turn-based RPG centered on recruiting a band of party members, traveling across multiple themed regions, and progressing through a story campaign. The combat loop is explicitly party-based and tactical, with timing-based rhythm inputs layered onto turn-based encounters via musical mashup attacks and changing combat conditions. This supports a JRPG-story-RPG identity first, with tactical RPG elements secondary. The rhythm component appears additive to the core party-managed campaign RPG structure rather than defining the game as a standalone rhythm genre.

overall_note: The rail has several solid JRPG comps, especially games with turn-based party combat and active timing inputs. It is weaker on exact musical-rhythm JRPG parallels, and one candidate leans too much on theme over combat structure.
anchor_summary: People of Note reads as a party-based, authored single-player JRPG with turn-based battles enhanced by timed rhythm inputs, team synergies, dungeon exploration, and a strong presentation layer built around music. Best matches should preserve the story-driven JRPG campaign loop and active-input turn-based combat rather than just sharing musical style.
gap_notes: The set covers turn-based story JRPGs well but is light on closer rhythm-input RPG comparisons.
A stronger rail would emphasize games where timed or performance-based inputs are central to each combat round.
One candidate appears included mainly for musical framing despite a more strategy-RPG combat structure.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Very strong fit: authored party-based JRPG with turn-based battles, exploration, and timed button presses that make combat actively interactive in a way close to the anchor.
Another close match on story-driven party RPG structure and action-command turn-based combat, though the available text gives less explicit combat detail than Super Mario RPG.
Strong modern comp for turn-based party RPG combat blended with real-time inputs, build synergies, and authored campaign progression, even if its tone and challenge profile are more intense.

Good broader JRPG match for party management, progression, dungeon runs, and strong authored presentation, but its social/calendar loop makes it structurally less direct than the active-input battle comps.
Understandable because of the musical story framing, but the isometric strategy-RPG battle structure pulls it away from the anchor’s more traditional party-based turn-based JRPG identity.

title: Hidden around the World

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Store and aggregator descriptions consistently identify the game as a cozy hidden-object adventure built around exploring hand-drawn city scenes, finding hidden objects, completing relaxing missions, unlocking new scenes, and decorating locations. The gameplay loop is discovery/puzzle-oriented rather than combat- or platform-focused, with single-player progression across handcrafted destinations. Metacritic also explicitly labels the genre as Hidden Object, reinforcing hidden_object_puzzle as the best fit.

overall_note: This candidate set is tightly aligned with the anchor's hidden-object identity. The best matches emphasize calm scene-scanning, hand-drawn environments, progression through stages, and in several cases creation/sharing features that strongly mirror the anchor's level-sharing hook. The only looser fit is the more point-and-click adventure-leaning Please, Touch The Artwork 2.
anchor_summary: Cozy single-player hidden-object puzzle/adventure centered on scanning hand-drawn scenes, completing relaxed find missions, unlocking new locations, adding light decoration/customization, and supporting user-created level sharing.
gap_notes: A direct must-keep comparison is present in Hidden in My Paradise, from the same creators and with nearly identical find/decorate/share structure.
The rail skews toward pure hidden-scene search games; that is appropriate for the anchor's core loop.
Only one candidate is meaningfully adjacent rather than tightly matched: Please, Touch The Artwork 2 adds more adventure/puzzle tasking beyond straightforward hidden-object scanning.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Closest match: same developer, same cozy hidden-object loop, hand-crafted scenes, mission-based progression, decoration, and user level creation/sharing.
Very strong fit on relaxed hidden-object scene scanning, hand-drawn levels, stage progression, hints, and map editor/community sharing.

Nearly as close as the first Hidden Through Time: cozy hidden-object search across hand-drawn scenes with campaign progression and robust create/share features, though its extra mechanics make it slightly less direct.

Shares cozy hidden-object discovery in illustrated scenes, but leans more into point-and-click adventure tasks, item collection, and casual puzzle solving than the anchor's purer hidden-scene loop.

title: A Rat's Quest: The Way Back Home - Season 1

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored Steam and Metacritic copy consistently describe a single-player, narrative-driven action adventure centered on movement and navigation: 'climb, jump, crawl, slither, and sneak,' with explicit 'hybrid gameplay' switching between side and top-down perspectives. Combat is clearly present but not dominant over traversal, with a sword, throwing daggers, survival against threats, and 'challenging boss encounters.' The game is framed as a cinematic authored journey with voiced cutscenes, supporting a long-form linear campaign. While the cinematic framing is strong, the core playable identity in the evidence is platform-navigation-led action with stealth elements, making action_platformer the best fit.

overall_note: The set captures the anchor's small-creature scale, authored adventure structure, and traversal-plus-stealth emphasis reasonably well, but several picks drift toward puzzle/VR, heavy combat RPG, or low-challenge exploration rather than the anchor's action-platforming core.
anchor_summary: Single-player cinematic action-platformer/action-adventure with a tiny rodent protagonist, hybrid side/top-down traversal, climbing/crawling/sneaking through oversized spaces, light-to-moderate combat with boss fights, and a linear story-driven campaign.
gap_notes: There is no near-identical hybrid 2D/overhead cinematic action-platformer in the set, so the rail leans on broader tiny-hero action-adventures.
Some candidates match the miniature-animal perspective well but miss the anchor's combat/challenge profile or stealth-platforming structure.
The strongest fits are the games centered on vulnerable small protagonists navigating dangerous oversized spaces with stealth, traversal, and story focus.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Tiny rodent-led single-player action-adventure with strong stealth, agile navigation, dangerous oversized spaces, and a story quest; less platformer-structured and more immersive sim/RPG, but very close in audience and play feel.

Very close on cinematic traversal through hostile oversized environments, squeezing/climbing/hiding, and linear tension-driven progression; weaker on direct combat and lacks the rodent/adventure-romance identity.

Shares storybook tiny-hero action-adventure, traversal, combat, and environmental navigation, but VR framing and player-assist puzzle structure make its core loop meaningfully different from the anchor's standard action-platformer campaign.

Matches side-scrolling small-rat action, dangerous creatures, and boss-centric progression, but it is much more combat-heavy, RPG-geared, and punishing than the anchor's traversal-stealth-cinematic balance.

Good miniature-animal perspective and narrative exploration overlap, but it is a gentle quest/puzzle adventure with little combat, little stealth tension, and no tough action-platformer challenge model.

title: All Will Fall

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify All Will Fall as a 'survival city builder/colony sim' centered on physics-based 3D construction, resource management, logistics, production chains, societal needs, research progression, random events, and sandbox/scenario play. The core loop is building and optimizing a settlement rather than commanding armies or pursuing direct combat. Overhead strategy presentation, base/colony growth, systemic simulation, and optimization pressure strongly support city_builder as the primary archetype, with colony_sim and factory_builder as secondary overlaps due faction needs, survival management, and supply-chain emphasis.

overall_note: The rail is directionally strong for survival city-builder fans, with good coverage of colony management, pressure, and scenario-driven settlement optimization. Its main gap is that only Timberborn closely reflects the anchor’s standout 3D/vertical construction and systemic building-physics identity; the rest lean more toward broader survival city-building than that specific structural sandbox niche.
anchor_summary: All Will Fall is a single-player survival city builder/colony sim focused on overhead settlement construction, resource logistics, faction/population needs, research, scenario/sandbox play, and a distinctive physics-based vertical building system where structural collapse is a core challenge.
gap_notes: The set underrepresents games with comparable vertical/stacked construction as a primary mechanic.
Only one candidate meaningfully echoes the anchor’s physics-driven building and collapse considerations.
Most candidates fit survival colony management well, but several are looser on sandbox construction freedom and logistics-led city design.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Very close on survival city-building, vertical architecture, systemic resource chains, faction differences, and environmental simulation; especially strong because 3D construction and water/terrain systems mirror the anchor’s structural-planning appeal.

Excellent match for scenario-driven survival city management, population needs, harsh tradeoffs, and difficult policy decisions, though it is less of a freeform construction sandbox and lacks the anchor’s vertical physics-building focus.

Good fit through pressure-heavy city/colony management, infrastructure balancing, exploration, and survival decision-making, but its closed habitat structure and space-station management are less construction-sandbox-driven than the anchor.

Solid post-apocalyptic colony builder with research, expeditions, disasters, and population management, but it is more conventional settlement survival and less defined by vertical building, physics, or dense logistics construction.

title: SHINOBI: Art of Vengeance - SEGA Villains DLC

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Accepted via DLC inheritance from the parent game because the DLC is clearly additive rather than mode-shifting: it adds 5 stages, 3 bosses, ninpos, skins, music, and boss rush modes. The parent game's stored description explicitly defines the core gameplay as 'an all-new 2D action platformer' with precision ninja combat, combos, obstacle-overcoming tools, challenging platforming puzzles, hidden secrets, and multiple stages. That strongly supports action_platformer with side-scrolling 2D stage-based progression, dominant combat, platform traversal, boss encounters, and precision platforming. The DLC's boss-rush/stage content reinforces rather than contradicts that identity.

overall_note: The set has the essential base-game pairing and several credible 2D ninja/action-platformer neighbors. The biggest weakness is structural drift: some candidates lean more toward metroidvania exploration or stealth than the anchor’s combat-forward stage-based action-platformer identity.
anchor_summary: A DLC expansion for a 2D single-player ninja action-platformer built around side-scrolling stages, precision combat, traversal challenges, boss fights, and additive progression content like new stages, bosses, ninpo, and boss rush.
gap_notes: The base game is correctly included and should be mandatory for a DLC anchor like this.
The best neighbors are 2D ninja action-platformers with combat, bosses, and precision traversal; Cyber Shadow and The Messenger fit that well.
Strider is a solid comp but is looser because its interconnected metroidvania structure is less aligned with the anchor’s stage-based progression.
Mark of the Ninja: Remastered is understandable by theme and perspective, but stealth-first play makes it a weaker similar-games rail fit.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Direct parent game of the DLC and the clearest gameplay match: same 2D ninja action-platforming, combat toolkit, stage design, bosses, and progression systems.
Very close fit as a 2D ninja action-platformer with combat-heavy stages, precise movement, boss encounters, and unlockable abilities that reinforce the same audience expectation.
Another fast 2D ninja action-platformer with tight controls, enemy-filled traversal, upgrades, bosses, and branching paths, though it broadens into a more expansive adventure structure.

Shares side-scrolling combat, acrobatic movement, and sword-focused action, but its massive interconnected metroidvania world is a looser structural fit than the anchor’s stage-based action-platformer design.

title: Fishbowl

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions repeatedly and explicitly call Fishbowl a 'visual novel' and a narrative slice-of-life coming-of-age story. The core loop centers on making choices over the course of a month, video-calling friends/family/coworkers, doing daily care/work tasks, and sorting through belongings and memory-related puzzles. The text stresses 'no bad endings, just different paths' and says 'the choices you make will shape the possibilities in Alo's future,' which strongly supports authored branching and dialogue/relationship-focused interaction. No combat is described anywhere; instead the gameplay is framed as intimate, cozy, grief-focused narrative progression with light puzzle/matching activities. That combination fits visual_novel much better than broader adventure labels.

overall_note: The proposed rail has a solid grasp of Fishbowl's intimate narrative, cozy slice-of-life tone, and memory/grief focus. Its best candidates match the anchor's low-pressure, story-first structure, but several picks drift toward broader emotional-adventure or domestic-puzzle neighbors rather than close gameplay identity matches.

title: Clean Up Earth

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently describes a relaxing environmental restoration/cleanup game focused on vacuuming waste, recycling materials, rebuilding damaged structures, exploring large maps, and cooperating with up to 25 players. The strongest supported traits are multiplayer/co-op support, large shared spaces, casual/simulation framing, and non-combat restorative play. However, the Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype list does not contain a suitable home for a cooperative cleanup/restoration exploration sim with minimal or no combat. The closest available archetypes are poor fits because they require identity-defining elements not supported here, such as farming/life loops, factory/engineering systems, colony/city management, survival pressures, shooter combat, or RPG progression. Because the gameplay identity is clear but falls outside the available archetype set, the title should be rejected rather than forced into an inaccurate category.

overall_note: The rail has a strong core around cleanup-and-restoration games with visible world recovery, but it skews heavily toward single-player neighbors and lacks a truly close multiplayer/community-scale comparator. The top picks fit the anchor’s core loop well, while the lower picks drift toward broader action-adventure or farming hybrids.
anchor_summary: Clean Up Earth is a relaxing restoration/cleanup sim built around vacuuming waste, recycling materials, rebuilding damaged areas, exploring maps, and watching immediate environmental recovery. Its identity is non-combat, tool-based cleanup with solo/co-op/community multiplayer support and large shared restoration spaces.
gap_notes: Most candidates match the cleanup/restoration fantasy but not the anchor’s notable co-op/community multiplayer profile.
